from_scratch.Question: In the board game Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), can pawn agents participate as attackers in religious wars, conspiracies, and peasant revolts?
from_scratch.A: Yes – pawn agents can participate as attackers in all three: religious wars, conspiracies, and peasant revolts.
from_scratch.B: No – pawn agents cannot participate as attackers in any of the three: religious wars, conspiracies, or peasant revolts.
from_scratch.C: Pawn agents can participate as attackers in conspiracies and peasant revolts, but not in religious wars.
from_scratch.D: Pawn agents can only participate as attackers in religious wars, but not in conspiracies or peasant revolts.
from_scratch.Explanation: Pawn agents cannot participate as attackers in religious wars according to the official rules, but they can participate in conspiracies and peasant revolts.

from_scratch.Question: In the board game Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), does the rule of paying $1 per attacker in a campaign also apply to repressed tokens acting as knights due to the 'slave soldiers' ability on the Mamluk card?
from_scratch.A: Yes – repressed tokens acting as knights must be paid for in a campaign, as they count as knights.
from_scratch.B: No – repressed tokens are considered slaves and are used in the campaign for free.
from_scratch.C: Yes – but only if you choose to use them as knights; otherwise, they remain free.
from_scratch.D: No – the 'slave soldiers' ability exempts repressed tokens from campaign costs.
from_scratch.Explanation: Repressed tokens acting as knights due to the 'slave soldiers' ability on the Mamluk card count as knights, so you have to pay for each of them when used in a campaign. The ability is not optional.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), does the rule of paying $1 per attacker in a campaign apply to repressed tokens acting as knights due to the 'slave soldiers' ability on the Mamluk card?
from_rulebook.A: Yes – repressed tokens acting as knights due to the 'slave soldiers' ability are considered knights, and you must pay for each of them when used in a campaign.
from_rulebook.B: Yes – repressed tokens acting as knights are considered knights, but you only pay for them if they are not used as casualties.
from_rulebook.C: No – repressed tokens acting as knights are considered slaves and can be used in a campaign for free.
from_rulebook.D: No – repressed tokens acting as knights are not considered as part of the campaign and do not require payment.
from_rulebook.Explanation: Repressed tokens acting as knights due to the 'slave soldiers' ability on the Mamluk card count as knights, so you have to pay for each of them when used in a campaign. They can also be used as casualties because they count as knights for the purpose of campaigning.
from_rulebook.Citation: Rulebook, Section F9: Campaign (Military Op)

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), what are the punch-out tokens that match a player's bank icon used for?

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), do repressed tokens count for victory conditions?
from_scratch.A: Yes – repressed tokens are considered for all victory conditions.
from_scratch.B: Yes – repressed tokens count towards the Supreme Religion for a Holy Victory.
from_scratch.C: No – repressed tokens do not count for any victory conditions.
from_scratch.D: No – only repressed tokens in the Border between two Empires count for victory conditions.
from_scratch.Explanation: Repressed tokens do not count for victory conditions. A Concession is defined as a Pawn located in the Border between two Empires, which means it is not repressed. Additionally, once repressed, a concession becomes a serf. For determining the Supreme Religion for a Holy Victory, only unrepressed Tokens whose color matches the Theocracy they live in are counted.

from_rulebook.Explanation: Repressed tokens do not count for victory conditions. For determining the Supreme Religion for a Holy Victory, only unrepressed Tokens whose color matches the Theocracy they live in are counted.
from_rulebook.Citation: Rulebook, Section A1: Summary

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), the Player's Guide mentions selling a royal couple or an old maid for 4 Florins to buy a card in the 5-cost spot. However, selling an old maid only yields 2 Florins. Is this a mistake or is there a specific rule or intention behind this?
from_scratch.A: Yes, it is a mistake in the rules.
from_scratch.B: No, the intention is to allow liquidation of assets for immediate capital, not necessarily profit.
from_scratch.C: Yes, but it is meant to be a rare exception in the game.
from_scratch.D: No, selling an old maid actually yields 4 Florins under specific conditions.
from_scratch.Explanation: The intention behind the Surprise Sell-Out strategy is not necessarily to make a profit but to liquidate assets to gain capital immediately. Selling something unexpected, like an old maid, can give you more reach into the market, even if it doesn't yield a direct profit.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), regarding the rules: 1) Does a discarded Empire Square retain its Vassal? 2) Do victorious adjacent crusading Knights move into the Map Card they helped win the battle in, if there are empty cities? 3) Can the Cryptography Card ability be used each time you perform West Ops?
from_scratch.A: 1) Yes, a discarded Empire Square retains its Vassal. 2) Yes, victorious Knights move into the Map Card. 3) Yes, the ability can be used each time you perform West Ops.

from_scratch.D: 1) No, a discarded Empire Square does not retain its Vassal. 2) Yes, victorious Knights move into the Map Card. 3) No, the ability is used only when the card is sold.
from_scratch.Explanation: 1) A discarded Empire Square does not retain its Vassal; both are returned to their Thrones. 2) Victorious Knights do not move into the Map Card; they return home after the battle. 3) The Cryptography Card ability is used only when sold, as per the card text.

from_rulebook.Question: In the board game Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), what does the Holy Victory condition mean in terms of bishops and tokens?
from_rulebook.A: It requires that a religion has more bishops and tokens than the other two religions combined.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), what are the correct rules for regime change when it happens to a card in a player's tableau?
from_scratch.A: 1) A card on the KING side in an opponent's tableau comes into your tableau on its REPUBLIC side. 2) A card on the REPUBLIC side in an opponent's tableau comes into your tableau on its KING side. 3) A card on the KING side in your own tableau becomes a REPUBLIC. 4) A card on the REPUBLIC side in your own tableau stays as a REPUBLIC.
from_scratch.B: 1) A card on the KING side in an opponent's tableau comes into your tableau on its KING side. 2) A card on the REPUBLIC side in an opponent's tableau comes into your tableau on its KING side. 3) A card on the KING side in your own tableau becomes a REPUBLIC. 4) A card on the REPUBLIC side in your own tableau returns to its KING side.
from_scratch.C: 1) A card on the KING side in an opponent's tableau comes into your tableau on its REPUBLIC side. 2) A card on the REPUBLIC side in an opponent's tableau comes into your tableau on its REPUBLIC side. 3) A card on the KING side in your own tableau stays as a KING. 4) A card on the REPUBLIC side in your own tableau returns to its KING side.
from_scratch.D: 1) A card on the KING side in an opponent's tableau stays in their tableau as a KING. 2) A card on the REPUBLIC side in an opponent's tableau stays in their tableau as a REPUBLIC. 3) A card on the KING side in your own tableau stays as a KING. 4) A card on the REPUBLIC side in your own tableau stays as a REPUBLIC.
from_scratch.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when a regime change occurs, a card on the KING side in an opponent's tableau comes into your tableau on its KING side, and a card on the REPUBLIC side in an opponent's tableau comes into your tableau on its KING side. A card on the KING side in your own tableau becomes a REPUBLIC, and a card on the REPUBLIC side in your own tableau returns to its KING side. Additionally, if you sell a King card that has Vassals, they do return to their own Thrones.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), what happens to a card on the KING side in an opponent's tableau when a regime change is triggered by a successful vote or a revolt/conspiracy/war?
from_rulebook.A: The card is discarded and the player who triggered the regime change gains a new card from the market.
from_rulebook.B: The card comes into your tableau on its KING side.
from_rulebook.C: The card is flipped to its REPUBLIC side and stays in the opponent's tableau.
from_rulebook.D: The card is removed from the game and replaced with a new card from the discard pile.
from_rulebook.Explanation: When a regime change is triggered on a card on the KING side in an opponent's tableau, the card is moved into your tableau on its KING side.
from_rulebook.Citation: Rulebook, Section E2.1: Play Hand Card & Place Agents

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), what are the correct rules for regime change when it happens to a card in a player's tableau?

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), do Knights in adjacent kingdoms also act as attackers in Campaigns, or is it just those Knights that reside in the kingdom doing the attacking?

from_scratch.D: To balance the game by making it harder for players to dominate the board.

from_rulebook.B: To maintain thematic consistency, as attacking one's own allies is seen as thematically inconsistent.

from_rulebook.D: To encourage players to focus on external conflicts and alliances rather than internal management.

from_scratch.A: Always choose the first option available to ensure consistency.

from_scratch.Explanation: In cases where the AI's choices are not clear, it is generally best to choose the option that is most beneficial for the AI. If this is not clear, a random choice among the options is recommended. This approach helps maintain the integrity of the solo play experience without requiring the player to make decisions for the AI.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), during a religious battle (Jihad) involving 3 knights from the Ottoman Empire and 1 knight from Byzantium against 2 tokens in Hungary, how should the placement of knights be handled after the battle?
from_scratch.A: The knights move to Hungary, taking control of the region.
from_scratch.B: The knights remain in their original countries, and the regime in Hungary changes with 0 tokens left.
from_scratch.C: The Ottoman knights move to Byzantium, and the Byzantium knight moves to Hungary.
from_scratch.D: All knights are eliminated, and the region remains under the control of the original empire.
from_scratch.Explanation: Knights and rooks never move from one empire to another as a result of a religious war or campaign. In this scenario, if the Jihad is in Hungary, you would eliminate 2 of the attackers and the 2 defenders in Hungary. Tokens never move out of their empire as a result of a battle.

from_scratch.Question: In the board game Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if you perform a successful vote or civil war on a republic, does it reverse back to the golden side and become an empire?
from_scratch.A: Yes – a successful vote or civil war on a republic can flip it back to an empire.
from_scratch.B: No – once a republic is established, it remains a republic regardless of votes or civil wars.
from_scratch.C: Yes – but only if the republic is not in your Tableau when the vote or civil war occurs.
from_scratch.D: No – a successful vote or civil war has no effect on the status of a republic.
from_scratch.Explanation: According to the rules: E4.e states that a victorious Strawman vote against your own King creates a Republic and vice versa. F6.c indicates that if it is in your Tableau, you flip it to its other side (King or Republic). J.d explains that if victorious in a civil war, you will gain a Concession and if the Empire Square is in your Tableau, you flip it to its other side (King or Republic).

from_scratch.Question: In the game Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), regarding the rule 'E2. PLAY HARD CARD & PLACE AGENTS, 1. If One-Shot Occurs. b. No Battle', are you required to place agents even if all cities or borders are occupied, and must you pay 1 florin to China to suppress existing tokens and place your own? Additionally, if you don't have florins, does this mean you cannot use 'One-Shot Occurs. No Battle'?
from_scratch.A: Yes, you must place agents regardless of occupation and pay 1 florin to China; without florins, you cannot use the action.
from_scratch.B: No, you are not required to place agents if all cities or borders are occupied, and the action can be used without paying florins.
from_scratch.C: Yes, you must place agents, but you do not need to pay 1 florin to China; the action can be used even without florins.
from_scratch.D: No, you can choose not to place agents if all cities or borders are occupied, and you can use the action without paying florins.
from_scratch.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance, placing agents is mandatory even if all cities or borders are occupied, requiring suppression by paying 1 florin to China. Without the florin, the 'One-Shot Occurs. No Battle' action cannot be used.

from_rulebook.Question: In the game rules for 'E2. PLAY HARD CARD & PLACE AGENTS, 1. If One-Shot Occurs. b. from_rulebook.A: Yes, you are required to place agents even if all cities or borders are occupied, and suppression is mandatory, requiring you to pay 1 florin to China. If you cannot pay the florin, you cannot use the 'One-Shot Occurs. No Battle' action.

from_rulebook.D: No, the placement of agents is optional, and you can choose not to pay the florin to China if you don't have enough funds.
from_rulebook.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance, the rules specify that when performing the 'One-Shot Occurs. No Battle' action, you must place agents even if all cities or borders are occupied, and you are required to pay 1 florin to China to suppress existing tokens. from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if you run a successful vote operation and turn a kingdom into a Republic in someone else's tableau, where does the new Republic go?

from_scratch.B: The Republic goes into your tableau on its Republic side.

F6c further clarifies that if the Empire Square is in an opponent’s tableau, you gain it as a King.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if at the end of a player's turn there is one deck and one card remaining to fill two spaces in the market, does this activate a patron victory?
from_scratch.A: Yes – the market refreshes automatically, activating a Patron Victory.
from_scratch.B: Yes – since the market cannot be fully refreshed, it triggers a Patron Victory.

from_scratch.Explanation: The market technically did not refresh because there is still a gap after the one card has been used, and so the market is still in need of refreshment. Since the cards have run out, this triggers a Patron Victory by rule L2a.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), can the abilities of Card A ('May do east & west ops in one action') and Card B ('you may activate eastern ops without expending an action') be combined to allow a player to activate eastern ops without expending an action and then use the benefits of Card A to do west ops as well?
from_scratch.A: Yes – combining the abilities allows you to perform both east and west ops without expending an action.
from_scratch.B: Yes – you can first activate eastern ops without expending an action and then use Card A to perform west ops.
from_scratch.C: No – you cannot combine the abilities in such a way because you cannot run east ops twice in one turn.
from_scratch.D: No – the abilities are mutually exclusive and cannot be used together in a single turn.
from_scratch.Explanation: The abilities of Card A and Card B cannot be combined in such a way. Once you have used the 'east ops without expending an action' ability, the 'east & west ops in one action' ability is effectively the same as just running west ops normally. The special abilities do not supersede the restriction that you can only run west ops once per turn and east ops once per turn.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), can the abilities of Card A ('May do east & west ops in one action') and Card B ('you may activate eastern ops without expending an action') be combined to allow a player to activate eastern ops without expending an action and then use the benefits of Card A to do west ops as well?
from_rulebook.A: Yes – you can combine both abilities to perform east ops without using an action and then west ops in the same action.
from_rulebook.B: Yes – the abilities allow you to bypass the restriction of performing east and west ops once per turn.
from_rulebook.C: No – the abilities cannot be combined because you cannot perform east ops twice in one turn.
from_rulebook.D: No – using Card B's ability to perform east ops without an action nullifies Card A's ability for that turn.
from_rulebook.Explanation: The abilities of Card A and Card B cannot be combined to perform both east and west ops without expending an action because the rules restrict players to performing east and west ops once per turn. Using the 'activate eastern ops without expending an action' ability means that the 'east & west ops in one action' ability is effectively the same as just running west ops normally.

from_rulebook_and_forum.Explanation: The abilities of Card A and Card B cannot be combined in such a way because the game rules restrict players to running east ops once and west ops once per turn. from_rulebook_and_forum.Citation: Rulebook, Section D1: Action Phase; Forum discussion by username 'Arcturian' on 2022-09-12

from_scratch.Question: In the board game Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), does the special ability 'Suleyman Patronage' on the card Zionist State mean that Green Pirates always count as red units, or do they need to be on a border to a reformist theocracy?
from_scratch.A: Green Pirates always count as red units regardless of their position.
from_scratch.B: Green Pirates count as red Bishops when calculating religious supremacy.
from_scratch.C: Green Pirates need to be on a border to a reformist theocracy to count as red units.
from_scratch.D: Green Pirates count as red units only when they are on a border to a reformist theocracy and have already been counted as Bishops.
from_scratch.Explanation: Green Pirates count as red Bishops when calculating religious supremacy. Additionally, when counting tokens in a reformist Theocracy, Green Pirates on their border also count as red units, even if they have already been counted as Bishops.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when a token is repressed or a bishop is placed, can you choose whether to place the tokens onto the Empty Throne or the Empire Square, and how does this affect the silencing of a king?
from_scratch.A: Yes, you can choose where to place the tokens, but the Empty Throne and Empire Square are treated as separate entities affecting the game's outcome.
from_scratch.B: No, tokens must always be placed on the Empire Square, and the throne is only for display purposes.
from_scratch.C: Yes, you can choose where to place the tokens, and the Empty Throne and Empire Square are treated as the same entity, affecting the game equally.
from_scratch.D: No, tokens must always be placed on the Empty Throne, and the Empire Square is only for display purposes.
from_scratch.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance, the throne and the empire square are treated as the same entity. It is just a preference of where to display the tokens. If a bishop is on the throne, it is considered to be on the empire square, and vice versa. Repressed tokens can be stored in the throne slot of their Empire Square when acquired into a tableau, but for all game purposes, they are still treated as though they were on their respective Empire Square.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when a token is repressed, or a bishop is placed, can I choose whether to place the tokens onto the Empty Throne or the Empire Square (which would be in a player's tableau)? from_rulebook.A: Yes, the throne and the empire square are treated as the same entity. It is just a preference of where to display the tokens.
from_rulebook.B: No, tokens on the throne and empire square are treated differently, affecting gameplay and silencing.
from_rulebook.C: Yes, but tokens placed on the throne do not affect the game state, while those on the empire square do.
from_rulebook.D: No, placing tokens on the throne is not allowed, they must be placed on the empire square only.
from_rulebook.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance, the throne and the empire square are treated as the same entity, and it is just a preference of where to display the tokens. If a bishop is on the throne, it is considered to be on the empire square, and vice versa.
from_rulebook.Citation: Rulebook, Section B4: Empire Squares

from_scratch.Question: In the board game Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), during the Blue turn, if you want to play a card with a One-shot ability involving a Knight, how does it resolve?
from_scratch.A: Place my Agent token first, repressing a yellow concession, then activate One-shot: my pawn (Agent) + 1 yellow repressed token vs Knight = Peasant Revolt success, I get Papal States.
from_scratch.B: Activate One-shot first: my pawn Agent vs Knight = 1 kill per part, no Revolt success.
from_scratch.C: Place my Agent token and activate One-shot simultaneously, leading to a successful Peasant Revolt without any casualties.
from_scratch.D: Activate One-shot first, leading to a successful Peasant Revolt and gaining control over the Papal States.
from_scratch.Explanation: The correct resolution is Option B, assuming there is a Knight in Venice, making the battle 1:1. You trigger the One-shot, placing the agent as an attacker and resolving the battle immediately with 1:1 casualties. If there are surviving attackers, they must go to an open space, or they are repressed for free if no spaces are available. You do not win the Peasant Revolt in this scenario.

from_rulebook.C: Activate One-shot first, but place my Agent and resolve the battle after the Knight's movement.
from_rulebook.D: Place my Agent, then activate One-shot to resolve the battle with an additional Knight from my reserve.
from_rulebook.Explanation: The correct resolution is Option B, assuming there is a Knight in Venice, making the battle 1:1. You trigger the One-shot, placing the agent as an attacker and resolving the battle immediately with 1:1 casualties. If there are surviving attackers, they must go to an open space, or they are repressed for free if no spaces are available. You do not win the Peasant Revolt in this scenario.

from_scratch.Question: In the board game Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), how does the card ability 'Your concessions cannot be killed by pirates' interact with pirate placement and movement?
from_scratch.A: A pirate cannot be moved onto your concession.

from_scratch.C: Pirates can move onto your concession, but they must leave before a trade fair.
from_scratch.D: Your concession is completely immune to pirates, allowing placement of new concessions even if pirates are present.
from_scratch.Explanation: A pirate placed on your concession coexists with it but cannot participate in trade fairs. However, you are still not allowed to place a new concession into a border containing a pirate.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), does the ability 'Your concessions cannot be killed by pirates' protect all your concessions or only those within Ottoman borders?

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), there is an Ottoman card with the ability 'Your concessions cannot be killed by pirates'. Are all my concessions immune to pirates or only those that stay in Ottoman borders?
from_rulebook.A: All concessions are immune to pirates, regardless of their location, as long as the ability is unsilenced and in your Tableau.

from_rulebook.D: Only concessions outside the Ottoman borders are immune to pirates, as the ability applies globally except for the Ottoman Empire.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when a Vassal returns to its Throne due to its Suzerain undergoing a Regime Change, are the Queen and Bishop retained or discarded, and are Repressed Tokens affected by this change? Is the return considered a Regime Change allowing for Emancipation?
from_scratch.A: The Queen and Bishop are discarded, Repressed Tokens are affected, and it is considered a Regime Change allowing for Emancipation.
from_scratch.B: The Queen and Bishop are retained, Repressed Tokens are not affected, and it is not considered a Regime Change, so no Emancipation occurs.
from_scratch.C: The Queen and Bishop are discarded, Repressed Tokens are not affected, but it is considered a Regime Change allowing for Emancipation.
from_scratch.D: The Queen and Bishop are retained, Repressed Tokens are affected, and it is considered a Regime Change, allowing for Emancipation.
from_scratch.Explanation: When a Vassal returns to its Throne due to its Suzerain undergoing a Regime Change, the Queen and Bishop are retained because the Vassal itself does not experience a Regime Change. from_scratch.Question: In the board game Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if a Reformist Theocracy is established in Hungary and then conquered by Catholic knights through a conspiracy, does the Reformist Theocracy remain?
from_scratch.A: Yes – the Reformist Theocracy remains because conspiracies are not religiously motivated, so the religion of the pieces does not impact the result.
from_scratch.B: Yes – but only if a religious war one-shot or a vote occurs immediately after the conspiracy.
from_scratch.C: No – the Reformist Theocracy is removed because the Catholic knights' conspiracy automatically changes the religious state.
from_scratch.D: No – a conspiracy always results in the removal of any existing theocracy regardless of its religious affiliation.
from_scratch.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance 2nd Edition, the removal of theocracies after a conspiracy requires a vote operation (Golden liberty). Conspiracies themselves are not religiously motivated, so they do not affect the religious state.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if a Reformist Theocracy is established in Hungary and then conquered by Catholic knights through a conspiracy, does the Reformist Theocracy remain?

from_rulebook.B: Yes – but only if the conspiracy was initiated by a Reformist player, otherwise it is removed.
from_rulebook.C: No – the Reformist Theocracy is removed because the conspiracy was conducted by Catholic knights.
from_rulebook.D: No – any conspiracy, regardless of the initiator's religion, removes the existing Theocracy.
from_rulebook.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance 2nd Edition, conspiracies are not religiously motivated, so the religion of the pieces does not impact the result. The removal of theocracies after a conspiracy requires a vote operation (Golden liberty).

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), is it a legal move for one's pawn to be repressed on a kingdom card held in one's tableau, and how can the pawn be released? Additionally, where can Operations be conducted?
from_scratch.A: No, it is not a legal move for a pawn to be repressed on a kingdom card in your tableau. Pawns cannot be repressed there.
from_scratch.B: Yes, it is legal for a pawn to be repressed on a kingdom card in your tableau. It can be released by paying 1 florin at any time.
from_scratch.C: Yes, it is legal for a pawn to be repressed on a kingdom card in your tableau. It can only be released during a regime change, and Operations can be conducted anywhere on the board.
from_scratch.D: Yes, it is legal for a pawn to be repressed on a kingdom card in your tableau. It can only be released during a regime change, and Operations can only be conducted in the location specified on the card.
from_scratch.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), it is legal for a pawn to be repressed on a kingdom card in your tableau. Operations can only be conducted on the location specified on the card, with some cards allowing actions in any Empire in the East or West depending on the icon.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), do bishops of the same color kill each other when moved to the same card or empire in the game, and if so, what is the historical justification for this rule?
from_rulebook.A: Yes, bishops of the same color kill each other, justified by historical events like the Western Schism, illustrating infighting within a religious faction.
from_rulebook.B: No, bishops do not kill each other; instead, they coexist peacefully to symbolize the unity of religious factions.
from_rulebook.C: Yes, bishops of different colors kill each other when moved to the same card, representing the conflicts between different religious factions.
from_rulebook.D: No, bishops of the same color do not kill each other, as they represent the harmonious cooperation within the same religious faction.
from_rulebook.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance, bishops of the same color kill each other when moved to the same card or empire. This rule is thematically justified by historical events such as the Western Schism, which involved infighting within a religious faction, illustrating that two leaders of the same faction can be in conflict.
from_rulebook.Citation: Rulebook, Section F1: Inquisitor (Religious Op)

from_scratch.A: Yes – agents must be placed on the map before any actions, including repression, are taken.

from_scratch.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance, repression occurs after the battle. Agents fight in the battle if the one-shot is activated. Repressed tokens can be placed in the Empire after a successful battle.

from_scratch.Question: In the board game Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), can a bishop be placed on an unclaimed empire square directly in the map area, and how can it be moved with inquisition? What happens if there is no opponent tableau for placement, and can the bishop affect Republics?

from_scratch.B: They have the option of emancipation.
from_scratch.C: They stay put and return with the Vassal to its throne.
from_scratch.D: They are removed and placed in a separate pool for future use.
from_scratch.Explanation: Repressed tokens stay put and return with the Vassal to its throne. They are not discarded, and liberation is not considered a regime change.

from_rulebook.Question: In the context of Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), what happens to repressed tokens after liberation?
from_rulebook.A: They are discarded and removed from play.
from_rulebook.B: They have the option of emancipation and can be placed back on the map.
from_rulebook.C: They stay put and return with the Vassal to its throne.
from_rulebook.D: They are converted into victory points for the player who liberated them.
from_rulebook.Explanation: Repressed tokens stay put and return with the Vassal to its throne. They are not discarded, and liberation is not considered a regime change.
from_rulebook.Citation: Rulebook, Book I, Section A4: Changes from 1st Edition

from_scratch.Question: In the board game Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when selling a card from your hand, do you have to show which card it is, and is the discard pile open or hidden?
from_scratch.A: Yes, you must show the card, and the discard pile is open.
from_scratch.B: Yes, you must show the card, but the discard pile is hidden.
from_scratch.C: No, you do not have to show the card, but the discard pile is open.
from_scratch.D: No, you do not have to show the card, and the discard pile is hidden.
from_scratch.Explanation: According to the living rules on page 38, when cards are discarded as a result of selling, they are removed facedown from the game. Therefore, you do not have to show which card you sell, and the discard pile is hidden.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if you sell a card from your hand, do you have to show which card? Also, is the discard pile open or hidden?
from_rulebook.A: Yes, you must show which card you sell, and the discard pile is open to all players.
from_rulebook.B: Yes, you must show which card you sell, but the discard pile is hidden from other players.
from_rulebook.C: No, you do not have to show which card you sell, and the discard pile is hidden from other players.
from_rulebook.D: No, you do not have to show which card you sell, but the discard pile is open to all players.
from_rulebook.Explanation: When cards are discarded as a result of selling in Pax Renaissance, they are removed facedown from the game, meaning you do not have to show which card you sell, and the discard pile is hidden.
from_rulebook.Citation: Rulebook, Page 38: Living Rules

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if a conspiracy results in a 5 vs 1 battle at Caffa, and the defending knight dies along with one agent, what happens to the other repressed piece or the extra agents that can't be placed?
from_scratch.A: The repressed pieces remain repressed, and any surviving attackers that can't be placed die.
from_scratch.B: The repressed pieces become active, and any surviving attackers that can't be placed become repressed tokens.
from_scratch.C: The repressed pieces remain repressed, and any surviving attackers that can't be placed become repressed tokens.
from_scratch.D: The repressed pieces become active, and any surviving attackers that can't be placed die.
from_scratch.Explanation: In this scenario, the battle is actually a 4 vs 1, as the adjacent concession does not participate in a Conspiracy one-shot. Any surviving attackers that cannot be placed become repressed tokens on the empire card.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when a conspiracy is played in Byzantium with specific conditions, what happens to the repressed pieces and extra agents?
from_rulebook.A: The repressed pieces are freed, and the extra agents are placed in any available city.
from_rulebook.B: The repressed pieces remain repressed, and the extra agents that can't be placed become repressed tokens on the empire card.
from_rulebook.C: The repressed pieces are moved to adjacent borders, and the extra agents are discarded.
from_rulebook.D: The repressed pieces are converted to the attacker's color, and the extra agents are returned to the player's reserves.
from_rulebook.Explanation: In a conspiracy, the battle is resolved without the adjacent concession participating. Surviving attackers that cannot be placed become repressed tokens on the empire card.
from_rulebook.Citation: Rulebook, Section J1: Conspiracy One-shot

from_scratch.Question: In a 4-player game of Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if the China reserve runs out of money due to a lack of trade fairs and players selling cards for 2 florins, what should be done?
from_scratch.A: Take money from the East Fair card, which has a surplus of florins.
from_scratch.B: End the game immediately, as running out of money is a game-ending condition.
from_scratch.C: Pause the game until more florins are available from the bank.
from_scratch.D: Use something else as a coin substitute, as the amount of florins in the game is unlimited.
from_scratch.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), the amount of florins is unlimited. If the China reserve runs out of money, players can use substitutes for coins.

from_rulebook.Question: In a 4-player game of Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if the China reserve runs out of money due to a lack of trade fairs and players selling cards for 2 florins, what should be done? Is it acceptable to take money from the East Fair card, which has a surplus of florins?
from_rulebook.A: Yes – you can take money from the East Fair card if it has a surplus of florins.
from_rulebook.B: Yes – you can borrow florins from the East Fair card, but you must repay them later.
from_rulebook.C: No – the amount of florins in the game is unlimited, so you can use substitutes.
from_rulebook.D: No – you must wait until the next trade fair to replenish the China reserve.
from_rulebook.Explanation: The amount of florins in the game is unlimited. If the China reserve runs out of money, you can use something else as a coin substitute.
from_rulebook.Citation: Rulebook, Book I - Rules, Section B1: Game Contents

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when playing a Crusade card and deciding on a One-Shot, do you place Agents from the card on cities before or after the battle?
from_scratch.A: Before the battle, you must place all Agents on available cities.
from_scratch.B: After the battle, you place surviving Agents on available cities.
from_scratch.C: You place Agents on cities only if you win the battle.
from_scratch.D: Agents are placed on cities regardless of the battle outcome.
from_scratch.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance, you resolve the battle first and then place the surviving agents in cities afterwards, as clarified in the rulebook under E2.1 and the Glossary entry for BATTLE.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when playing the Holy Land Crusade card and performing a One-Shot Crusade on Mamluks, and there is only one free city available, can you proceed with the battle since you cannot place two knights on the map first?
from_rulebook.A: Yes – you must place agents from the card on cities before the battle, even if it means not all agents can be placed.
from_rulebook.B: No – you cannot proceed with the battle if you cannot place all agents on the map before the battle.
from_rulebook.C: Yes – you resolve the battle first and then place the surviving agents in cities afterwards.
from_rulebook.D: No – agents must be placed on the map before any battle can occur, regardless of city availability.
from_rulebook.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance, you resolve the battle first and then place the surviving agents in cities afterwards. If there are not enough free cities during placement, the Repress is free.
from_rulebook.Citation: Rulebook, Section E2.1 and Glossary entry for BATTLE

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when playing a Crusade card and deciding on a One-Shot, do you need to place Agents on cities before the battle, or are they placed after the battle if it is won?
from_forum.A: Agents must be placed on cities before the battle, otherwise the One-Shot is cancelled.
from_forum.B: Agents are placed on cities after the battle, only if the battle is won.
from_forum.C: Agents can be placed on cities either before or after the battle, depending on the player's choice.
from_forum.D: Agents are not placed on cities at all during a One-Shot Crusade.
from_forum.Explanation: You resolve the battle first and then place the surviving agents in cities afterwards. This is clarified in the English rulebook under E2.1 and the Glossary entry for BATTLE. Surviving attackers must be placed as a priority on empty cities after a One-Shot fight.
from_forum.Citation: English rulebook, E2.1 and Glossary entry for BATTLE; username: Arcturian, post_date: 2024-07-25T16:33:38-05:00

from_rulebook_and_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when playing a Crusade card and deciding on a One-Shot, do you need to place Agents from the card on cities before the battle, or are they placed after the battle if it is won?
from_rulebook_and_forum.A: Agents must be placed on cities before the battle starts, otherwise the One-Shot is cancelled.
from_rulebook_and_forum.B: Agents are placed on cities after the battle, but only if there are enough free cities available.
from_rulebook_and_forum.C: You resolve the battle first and then place the surviving agents in cities afterwards.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when organizing trade, can cities that are blocked locations of trade routes, such as the Spice Islands, be used as valid locations for placing a tower or a knight? Additionally, how is the border between the Byzantine Empire and the Mamluk Sultanate described?
from_scratch.A: Yes, blocked cities can be used for placing tokens, and the border is a maritime border.
from_scratch.B: No, blocked cities cannot be used for placing tokens, and the border is a maritime border.
from_scratch.C: Yes, blocked cities can be used for placing tokens, and the border is a terrestrial border.
from_scratch.D: No, blocked cities cannot be used for placing tokens, and the border is a terrestrial border.
from_scratch.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), an emporium with a disc on it (a 'busted emporium') cannot hold a token. The border between the Byzantine Empire and the Mamluk Sultanate is a land border, not a sea border, as a border is a sea border only if it's crossed by a trade route.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when organizing trade, do cities that are blocked locations of trade routes, such as the Spice Islands, count as valid locations for placing a tower or a knight? from_rulebook.A: Yes, blocked cities can hold a token and the border is maritime.
from_rulebook.B: No, blocked cities cannot hold a token and the border is terrestrial.
from_rulebook.C: Yes, blocked cities can hold a token and the border is terrestrial.
from_rulebook.D: No, blocked cities cannot hold a token and the border is maritime.
from_rulebook.Explanation: Blocked cities, or 'busted emporia', cannot hold tokens as covered in C1a and reiterated in the Glossary entry for EMPORIUM. The border between the Byzantine Empire and the Mamluk Sultanate is a land border, as a border is a sea border only if crossed by a trade route.

from_scratch.Question: In the board game Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), what happens to a Vassal that transforms into a Republic, specifically regarding the placement of the Republic card in the player's tableau?
from_scratch.A: The Republic card remains in the same position as the Vassal card.
from_scratch.B: The Republic card is moved to the rightmost position of the player's tableau.
from_scratch.C: The Republic card is moved to the leftmost position of the player's tableau.
from_scratch.D: The Republic card is removed from the tableau entirely.
from_scratch.Explanation: The living rules of Pax Renaissance state that when a Vassal transforms into a Republic, the Republic card is moved to the leftmost position of the player's tableau, as shown in the example on page 33 of the rulebook.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), what happens to a Vassal that transforms into a Republic?
from_rulebook.A: The Vassal remains in its position but changes its side to Republic, and no movement within the tableau occurs.
from_rulebook.B: The Republic card must be moved to the leftmost position of the player's tableau, indicating it is no longer a Vassal.
from_rulebook.C: The Vassal is flipped to its Republic side, and all its Repressed Tokens are automatically Emancipated.
from_rulebook.D: The Republic card is removed from the tableau and placed back into the Throne, as it can no longer be a Vassal.
from_rulebook.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance, when a Vassal becomes a Republic, the Republic card is moved to the leftmost position of the player's tableau to indicate it is no longer a Vassal. This is supported by the example on page 33 of the rulebook.
from_rulebook.Citation: Rulebook, Page 33; Living Rules, Civil War and Religious War sections

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), what happens to a Vassal that transforms into a Republic, as shown in the example on page 33 of the rulebook?
from_forum.A: The Republic card remains in its original position within the player's tableau.
from_forum.B: The Republic card is moved to the leftmost position in the player's tableau, indicating it is no longer a Vassal.
from_forum.C: The Republic card is removed from the game entirely.
from_forum.D: The Republic card is placed in the discard pile and a new card is drawn.
from_forum.Explanation: Although the printed rules do not specify this, the living rules clarify that when a Vassal transforms into a Republic, the Republic card should be moved to the leftmost position in the player's tableau.

from_rulebook_and_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), what happens to a Vassal that transforms into a Republic?
from_rulebook_and_forum.A: The Republic card remains in its current position within the tableau, as the rules do not specify a move.
from_rulebook_and_forum.B: The Republic card is moved to the leftmost position in the player's tableau, indicating it is no longer a Vassal.
from_rulebook_and_forum.C: The Republic card is removed from the game, as it can no longer participate in the player's strategy.
from_rulebook_and_forum.D: The Republic card is flipped back to a King, as Vassals cannot become Republics through any action.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition) on Boardgamearena, if you have The Last Sigh in your tableau with the power 'Leo Africanus: May do east and west ops in one action', can this be combined with Civil Engineer's power 'Fortifications: you may do 'activate eastern ops' without expending an action' to perform both east and west ops for free each turn?
from_scratch.A: Yes – combining these powers allows you to perform both east and west ops for free each turn.
from_scratch.B: Yes – the rules allow you to merge these abilities for combined free actions.
from_scratch.C: No – the abilities do not combine to allow both east and west ops for free.
from_scratch.D: No – while you can perform one of the ops for free, the other requires an action.
from_scratch.Explanation: No, the abilities do not combine to allow both east and west ops for free. The current definition of 'free ops' specifies that an ability running both East and West Ops as a single action runs them separately and concurrently, meaning the west ops part isn't free. Additionally, a side can only be activated once during a player's turn.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when a Regime Change occurs and a Concession is placed between two empires, where should the submitted pawn be placed if the Kingdom of Aragon enters play and the Kingdom of Castilla Y Portugal is also in play?
from_scratch.A: On the Kingdom of Aragon's empire card.
from_scratch.B: On the Kingdom of Castilla Y Portugal's empire card.
from_scratch.C: On the empire card of the kingdom that has more influence.
from_scratch.D: On the empire card of the kingdom with the most concessions.
from_scratch.Explanation: The submitted pawn should be placed on the empire card of the kingdom that is having the regime change, which in this example is Aragon.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when a Regime Change occurs and a Concession is placed between two empires, where should the submitted pawn be placed if the Kingdom of Aragon enters play and the Kingdom of Castilla Y Portugal is also in play?
from_rulebook.A: The submitted pawn should be placed on the empire card of the kingdom that is having the regime change, which in this example is Aragon.
from_rulebook.B: The submitted pawn should be placed on the Kingdom of Castilla Y Portugal, as it is the closest kingdom in play.
from_rulebook.C: The submitted pawn should be placed on the map between Aragon and Castilla Y Portugal, representing a shared concession.
from_rulebook.D: The submitted pawn should be placed in the player's reserve and not on the map, as the regime change does not affect pawn placement.
from_rulebook.Explanation: In Pax Renaissance, when a Regime Change occurs, the submitted pawn is placed on the empire card of the kingdom that is having the regime change.
from_rulebook.Citation: Rulebook, Section E2: Play Hand Card & Place Agents

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), does an opponent's card in their tableau that states declaring a Globalization victory costs two actions affect the victory declaration?
from_scratch.A: Yes – it requires you to spend two actions instead of one to declare a Globalization victory.
from_scratch.B: Yes – but only if the card is in the same row as the Globalization card.
from_scratch.C: No – the opponent's tableau does not impact your victory declaration.
from_scratch.D: No – the rule only applies if the opponent has more influence in the region.
from_scratch.Explanation: If your opponent has a card in their tableau that states declaring a Globalization victory costs two actions, you must spend two actions to declare the victory.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance, does an opponent's card in their tableau that states declaring a Globalization victory costs two actions affect the victory declaration?
from_rulebook.A: Yes – if your opponent has such a card, you must spend two actions instead of one to declare a Globalization victory.
from_rulebook.B: No – the card only affects the player who owns it, not their opponents.
from_rulebook.C: Yes – but only if the card is in the same tableau as the player attempting to declare the victory.
from_rulebook.D: No – the Globalization victory conditions are not affected by cards in opponents' tableaux.
from_rulebook.Explanation: The rules specify that if an opponent has a card in their tableau that states declaring a Globalization victory costs two actions, it affects the victory declaration process for others.
from_rulebook.Citation: Rulebook, Section E6: Victory Action; Golden Rule: If the text on a card contradicts these rules, the card has precedence.
